content.text: A retrospective without tracked action items is a venting session. The moment the room disperses, insights evaporate, "we should" statements turn into "someone will" assumptions, and the next launch inherits the same problems. Launch retrospective action items are the mechanism that converts retrospective insight into institutional improvement.

attrs.alt: Launch retrospective action item lifecycle: Retrospective → Write → Triage → Execute → Verify+Close, with four category bands and the fate of untracked items

content.text: The Retrospective Meeting Format

The retrospective is a 60-minute structured meeting held within one week of the 30-day post-launch review. Holding it close to the 30-day review ensures that the launch experience is still fresh but enough data has accumulated to distinguish real patterns from noise.
Agenda

## Launch Retrospective — [Feature Name]
**When:** Within 1 week of 30-day post-launch review
**Duration:** 60 minutes
**Attendees:** Feature team (engineers, PM, QA, SRE, design if applicable)
**Facilitator:** PM or a rotating neutral facilitator (not the feature lead)

### Setup (5 min)
- Facilitator reminds the group: retrospectives are blameless.
  We analyze systems and processes, not individual decisions.
  "We should have caught X" → "Our process didn't catch X; how do we fix the process?"

### 1. What went well (15 min)
- Silent individual write (5 min): each person writes 2–3 sticky notes
- Read-out and group (10 min): facilitator clusters duplicates on a shared board
- Purpose: identify what to preserve and amplify; things that worked should be
  explicitly codified, not just happen again by luck

### 2. What to improve (20 min)
- Silent individual write (5 min): each person writes 2–3 sticky notes
- Read-out and group (10 min): cluster by theme
- Root cause discussion (5 min): for the top 2–3 clusters, 5-why analysis
  to find the process or tooling gap, not the symptom

### 3. Surprises (10 min)
- What happened that nobody predicted?
- Were surprises positive (unexpected wins) or negative (unknown unknowns)?
- Which surprises indicate a gap in pre-launch planning?

### 4. Raw action item generation (10 min)
- From the "improve" and "surprise" clusters, the group nominates action items
- Each item must answer: what specifically changes, and for whom?
- The facilitator writes them in a shared doc; owners are NOT assigned yet
  (assignment happens after the meeting, preventing the loudest voice from
  defaulting to the most capable engineer)
Facilitator rule:

Never assign owners during the meeting. Assigning owners in the room leads to "volunteer capture" — the person who most recently spoke gets the item, regardless of fit. Owner assignment is done by the PM within 48 hours of the meeting, based on workload and expertise.
Writing Effective Action Items
Raw retrospective output is rarely action-item-ready. The PM converts raw "we should" statements into specific, measurable action items within 48 hours of the meeting.
The SMART Transformation
Raw retro statement
Problem with it
Transformed action item
We should have better eval coverage
Vague — no owner, no metric, no deadline
Add 50 eval cases covering multi-page PDF table extraction by 2026-08-01 (@qe-lead)
The deploy was too manual
No specificity — which steps? which tool?
Automate the prompt version bump step in the deploy pipeline using GitHub Actions by 2026-08-15 (@devops-eng)
Communication with support was bad
Two problems mixed (internal comms + docs)
(1) Write support runbook for feature X by 2026-07-25 (@support-lead); (2) Create pre-launch support briefing template by 2026-08-01 (@pm)
We need better monitoring
No scope — monitoring of what, alerting on what?
Add Prometheus alert for p99 latency > 3s on the /generate endpoint by 2026-07-22 (@sre-lead)
Action Item Schema

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from typing import Literal

ActionCategory = Literal[
    "process_gap",       # missing checklist, unclear ownership, review gap
    "technical_debt",    # prompt hacks, missing tests, eval gaps, runbook holes
    "tooling",           # deploy pipeline, automation, observability
    "knowledge_comms",   # docs missing, stakeholders not informed, KB gaps
]

@dataclass
class RetroActionItem:
    id: str
    feature_id: str
    category: ActionCategory
    title: str                       # verb phrase: "Add...", "Fix...", "Create..."
    description: str                 # what specifically changes and how you know it is done
    success_criteria: str            # measurable: "50 eval cases added to suite; CI runs them"
    owner: str                       # single person, not a team
    due_date: date
    priority: Literal["P1", "P2", "P3"]
    sprint: str | None = None        # e.g. "2026-Q3-S02"
    recurring: bool = False          # True if this item prevents recurrence (e.g. add to checklist)
    links: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)   # Jira tickets, PRs, docs

    def is_overdue(self) -> bool:
        from datetime import date as d
        return d.today() > self.due_date

    def to_jira_summary(self) -> str:
        return f"[Retro/{self.feature_id}] [{self.category}] {self.title}"

item = RetroActionItem(
    id="RETRO-2026-DOC-QA-003",
    feature_id="genai-document-qa-v2",
    category="technical_debt",
    title="Add eval cases for multi-page PDF table extraction",
    description=(
        "The 30-day review revealed that table extraction quality degrades when "
        "tables span page boundaries. There are currently 0 eval cases covering "
        "this scenario. Add at least 50 cases with ground-truth answers."
    ),
    success_criteria=(
        "50 multi-page table eval cases added to the eval suite; "
        "CI runs them on every merge to main; baseline score recorded."
    ),
    owner="qe-lead@company.com",
    due_date=date(2026, 8, 1),
    priority="P2",
    sprint="2026-Q3-S01",
    recurring=False,
)
Categorizing Action Items

Tagging action items by category enables trend analysis across launches. If five consecutive launches produce "process gap" items of the same type, that is a systemic problem requiring a program-level fix, not five individual action items.
Process Gap Items
Process gaps are the most common category. They represent steps that should have been on a checklist or review gate but were not.
Support team was not briefed before launch → add support briefing to the pre-launch checklist
No rollback plan was documented before go-live → add rollback plan as a required artifact in the launch readiness review
Privacy review was skipped for a new data field → add PII classification check to the design review template
On-call rotation was not updated before launch → add on-call update to the launch day checklist

Process gap items should modify a  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'checklist or template', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]}  — not just be fixed for this one launch. If the item does not change a reusable artifact, the same gap will appear in the next launch retrospective.
Technical Debt Items
Technical debt items are work that was deferred during the launch sprint to meet the ship date. They are expected; the failure mode is not tracking them and letting them accumulate.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class TechDebtItem:
    id: str
    description: str
    deferred_from: str                # "launch sprint" | "beta period" | "post-launch fix"
    interest_rate: str                # what gets worse over time if not addressed
    estimated_effort_days: float
    due_date: date
    owner: str
    blocking_graduation: bool = False

# Examples generated from a document-QA feature launch
debt_items = [
    TechDebtItem(
        id="TD-001",
        description="Prompt uses hardcoded date in system message instead of dynamic injection",
        deferred_from="launch sprint",
        interest_rate="Responses will reference stale dates; quality degrades gradually",
        estimated_effort_days=0.5,
        due_date=date(2026, 7, 22),
        owner="fe-engineer@company.com",
    ),
    TechDebtItem(
        id="TD-002",
        description="RAG chunk size chosen by trial-and-error; no systematic eval of alternatives",
        deferred_from="beta period",
        interest_rate="Suboptimal retrieval for table-heavy docs; blocks quality improvement",
        estimated_effort_days=3.0,
        due_date=date(2026, 8, 15),
        owner="ml-engineer@company.com",
    ),
    TechDebtItem(
        id="TD-003",
        description="Embedding model version pinned to deprecated release",
        deferred_from="launch sprint",
        interest_rate="Security risk; quality gap widens as new model improves",
        estimated_effort_days=2.0,
        due_date=date(2026, 7, 31),
        owner="platform-eng@company.com",
        blocking_graduation=True,
    ),
]
Tooling and Automation Items
Tooling items address manual steps that were painful during launch and should be automated before the next launch.
Manual step that was painful
Automation target
Benefit
Manually bumping prompt version in 3 config files
GitHub Action that updates all version references from a single source
Eliminates version drift; reduces deploy time by 20 min
Pulling eval scores by hand from 4 different dashboards
Automated weekly eval report emailed to team
Consistent data; PM can monitor without engineering help
Manually checking that all runbooks are linked in the on-call doc
Pre-merge CI check that validates runbook links
Catches gaps before they cause incident delays
Copy-pasting support ticket templates for common failure modes
Support macro library with auto-populated templates
Reduces support response time; consistent language
Knowledge and Communications Items
These items address gaps in documentation, stakeholder communication, or team knowledge transfer.
Support team escalated tickets that engineering could have answered from existing docs → write a support FAQ for the top 10 question categories
Sales team promised a feature capability that was not yet in GA → add sales enablement review to the launch readiness checklist
New team member needed 3 days to understand the prompt architecture → write a 30-minute onboarding doc for the prompt system
Post-launch runbook was accurate but used non-standard tool names → standardize tool references in all runbooks
Tracking and Accountability
Action Item Dashboard
Action items must be tracked in a persistent location that the whole team can see — not just the PM's notes. A shared Jira board filtered by the retrospective label is the standard approach.

import httpx
from datetime import date

def create_retro_action_items_in_jira(
    items: list["RetroActionItem"],
    jira_project: str,
    jira_base_url: str,
    jira_token: str,
    epic_key: str,     # the launch epic — all retro items link to it
) -> list[str]:
    """
    Creates Jira issues for each action item and links them to the launch epic.
    Returns the created issue keys.
    """
    priority_map = {"P1": "Critical", "P2": "Major", "P3": "Minor"}
    created_keys = []

    for item in items:
        payload = {
            "fields": {
                "project": {"key": jira_project},
                "summary": item.to_jira_summary(),
                "description": (
                    f"*Category:* {item.category}
"
                    f"*Description:* {item.description}
"
                    f"*Success criteria:* {item.success_criteria}
"
                    f"*Due date:* {item.due_date.isoformat()}
"
                    f"*Recurring item (modifies a template/checklist):* "
                    f"{'Yes' if item.recurring else 'No'}"
                ),
                "issuetype": {"name": "Task"},
                "priority": {"name": priority_map[item.priority]},
                "duedate": item.due_date.isoformat(),
                "assignee": {"name": item.owner},
                "labels": ["retrospective", f"feature-{item.feature_id}"],
            }
        }
        response = httpx.post(
            f"{jira_base_url}/rest/api/2/issue",
            json=payload,
            headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {jira_token}"},
        )
        key = response.json()["key"]
        created_keys.append(key)

        # Link to launch epic
        httpx.post(
            f"{jira_base_url}/rest/api/2/issueLink",
            json={
                "type": {"name": "is part of"},
                "inwardIssue": {"key": key},
                "outwardIssue": {"key": epic_key},
            },
            headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {jira_token}"},
        )

    return created_keys
Weekly Action Item Review
The PM reviews all open retrospective action items as part of the weekly feedback sync. Items approaching their due date without progress get a personal follow-up, not an automated Jira reminder.

import httpx
from datetime import date, timedelta

def get_overdue_and_at_risk_items(
    jira_project: str,
    feature_id: str,
    jira_base_url: str,
    jira_token: str,
    warning_days: int = 5,
) -> dict:
    """
    Returns items that are overdue or due within warning_days.
    Used in the weekly review to flag items needing PM follow-up.
    """
    today = date.today()
    warning_threshold = today + timedelta(days=warning_days)

    jql = (
        f"project = {jira_project} "
        f"AND labels = feature-{feature_id} "
        f"AND labels = retrospective "
        f"AND status != Done "
        f"ORDER BY duedate ASC"
    )
    response = httpx.get(
        f"{jira_base_url}/rest/api/2/search",
        params={"jql": jql, "fields": "summary,duedate,assignee,priority"},
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {jira_token}"},
    )
    issues = response.json()["issues"]

    overdue, at_risk = [], []
    for issue in issues:
        due_str = issue["fields"].get("duedate")
        if not due_str:
            continue
        due = date.fromisoformat(due_str)
        if due < today:
            overdue.append(issue)
        elif due <= warning_threshold:
            at_risk.append(issue)

    return {"overdue": overdue, "at_risk": at_risk}
Closing an Action Item

An action item is not closed when the work is done — it is closed when the  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'success criterion is verified by the reviewer', 'marks': [{'type': 'bold'}]} . This distinction prevents self-closing without evidence.

## Action Item Closure Checklist

Before marking RETRO-2026-DOC-QA-003 as Done:

### Owner confirms:
- [ ] Work is complete: 50 eval cases added to eval suite
- [ ] CI configuration updated: eval suite runs on every merge to main
- [ ] Baseline score recorded in the eval dashboard
- [ ] PR/commit link added to the Jira ticket

### Reviewer confirms (PM or QA lead):
- [ ] Pulled the eval suite and counted: 52 cases matching the specification ✅
- [ ] Triggered a test CI run: eval stage ran and produced results ✅
- [ ] Baseline score visible in dashboard: 4.21 / 5.0 recorded ✅

### If recurring item (modifies a checklist/template):
- [ ] The checklist or template has been updated
- [ ] Team has been notified of the change
- [ ] The change is visible in the permanent location (not just this ticket)

**Reviewer:** @qe-lead — 2026-08-03

The "if recurring item" section is the most important part of the closure checklist. An action item that fixes a gap for this launch but does not update the process to prevent the same gap in the next launch has delivered only 50% of its value.
Cross-Launch Trend Analysis
After three or more launches, retrospective action items become a dataset. Analyzing the dataset reveals systemic gaps that individual retrospectives cannot surface.

A  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'process_gap', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}  category appearing in 80% of launches with  {'type': 'text', 'text': 'recurring=False', 'marks': [{'type': 'code'}]}  on the closed items is the clearest signal of an organization failing to learn. The action items were completed, but the process never changed.
Summary
Launch retrospective action items turn retrospective insight into lasting improvement:
Blameless format
— analyze systems and processes, not individual decisions
Assign owners post-meeting
— prevents volunteer capture; PM assigns based on fit and workload within 48 hours
SMART transformation
— convert every raw 'we should' into a specific, measurable, owner-assigned, deadline-bound item
Four categories
— process gap, technical debt, tooling, knowledge/comms — tag every item for trend analysis
Recurring flag
— process gap items must update a checklist or template; a one-off fix that doesn't change the process delivers half the value
Closure requires evidence
— success criteria must be verified by a reviewer, not self-closed by the owner
Cross-launch analysis
— categories appearing in > 50% of launches signal systemic gaps that require program-level responses
